title: Attention Is All You Need: What the Original Transformer Actually Changed

central_claim.text: The original Transformer replaces sequence-aligned recurrence and convolution with stacked self-attention and feed-forward processing, enabling more parallel training while retaining autoregressive decoding.

tldr: The Transformer removes sequence-aligned recurrent and convolutional layers from the encoder-decoder and uses self-attention to connect token positions directly.
Scaled dot-product attention compares queries with keys, normalizes the scores, and mixes values; multiple heads repeat that operation in learned representation subspaces.

The model trained faster than the cited recurrent and convolutional systems on 2017 translation benchmarks, but full self-attention has quadratic sequence-length cost and target generation remains autoregressive.

Primary sources disagree on the English-to-French BLEU result: arXiv v7 reports 41.8 in its abstract and Table 2, while its prose and the proceedings PDF say 41.0 and the NeurIPS landing page says 41.1.

blocks.question: Why was a different sequence model needed?
paragraphs: Recurrent sequence models process positions through a chain of hidden states. That dependency makes computation within a training example inherently sequential, which limits parallelization as sequences grow. Convolutional sequence models can compute positions in parallel, but connecting distant positions requires paths that grow with their separation.

Attention already helped encoder-decoder systems retrieve information across a sequence, but it was usually combined with recurrence. The paper asks whether attention can replace sequence-aligned recurrence and convolution rather than merely supplement them.

blocks.question: What did the Transformer change in the encoder-decoder pattern?
paragraphs: The Transformer keeps an encoder-decoder structure but changes the operation used to exchange information between positions. Encoder positions use self-attention, decoder positions use masked self-attention over the known target prefix, and decoder queries attend to the encoder output.

This shortens the maximum path between positions to a constant number of sequential operations per self-attention layer. It does not make every part of the model attention: each layer also contains position-wise feed-forward networks, residual connections, layer normalization, embeddings, positional encodings, and an output projection with softmax.

blocks.question: How does one Transformer prediction work?
paragraphs: Tokens first become learned vectors, and sinusoidal position encodings are added so the model receives order information. The base encoder repeats 6 layers, each containing multi-head self-attention followed by a position-wise feed-forward network. Residual connections and layer normalization surround both sublayers.

For scaled dot-product attention, the model compares a query with every key using Q times K-transpose, divides by the square root of the key dimension, applies softmax, and uses the resulting weights to mix the values. The base model performs this calculation in 8 learned heads, concatenates their outputs, and projects the result.

The decoder also repeats 6 layers. Its self-attention masks future target positions, then encoder-decoder attention uses decoder queries with encoder keys and values. The final decoder representation passes through a linear projection and softmax to produce probabilities for one next token. The selected token is appended and decoding repeats.

blocks.question: What happens at one target position?
paragraphs: Take a decoder position whose preceding target tokens are known. Its query is compared with keys from the known target prefix. Future positions are masked before softmax, so they receive no attention weight. Dividing the query-key scores by the square root of the key dimension keeps large dot products from pushing softmax into regions with very small gradients.

After masked self-attention, the decoder position forms another query and compares it with keys from every encoded source position. The weighted source values provide input context. The decoder's feed-forward path and output projection then produce a next-token distribution. During training, known target positions can be evaluated in parallel under the mask; during generation, the model must append one selected token before predicting the next.

blocks.question: What did the authors test, and what were the critical results?
paragraphs: For WMT 2014 translation, the authors used about 4.5 million English-German sentence pairs with a shared 37,000-token byte-pair vocabulary and 36 million English-French sentences with a 32,000-token word-piece vocabulary. Training used one machine with 8 NVIDIA P100 GPUs. The base model trained for 100,000 steps or 12 hours; the big model trained for 300,000 steps or 3.5 days.

ArXiv v7 Table 2 reports 28.4 BLEU for the big English-German model and 41.8 for English-French. The 28.4 result exceeds the comparison ensembles in that table. The English-French value is internally inconsistent: v7 Section 6.1 says 41.0, as does the official proceedings PDF, while the NeurIPS landing abstract says 41.1. The result must therefore be cited by exact source version rather than flattened into one number.

The newstest2013 development ablation reports 25.8 BLEU for the base configuration, 24.9 with one attention head, 25.4 with 32 heads, and 25.7 when learned positional embeddings replace sinusoids. The v7 parsing extension reports F1 scores of 91.3 with the WSJ-only data and 92.7 with semi-supervised data, below the best listed result of 93.3.

blocks.question: What does the paper leave unresolved?
paragraphs: Full self-attention costs O(n squared times d) per layer. The paper argues it is faster than recurrence when sequence length n is smaller than representation dimension d, but that condition is not universal. For very long inputs, the authors propose restricted attention as future work, which would increase the maximum path length to O(n divided by r).

The experiments cover WMT translation and, in arXiv v7, English constituency parsing. They do not establish superiority across all language tasks, modalities, sequence lengths, or hardware. The paper reports no repeated-seed uncertainty, and its decoding hyperparameters were selected on development data.

Sinusoidal positional encodings are motivated by a hypothesis that they may extrapolate to longer sequences, but the reported comparison only shows near-equal in-distribution development performance against learned positions. Attention examples may show patterns, but the paper does not establish that attention weights are faithful causal explanations.

blocks.question: How should the title and historical claim be read?
paragraphs: The durable contribution is narrower and clearer than the title: the paper shows that sequence-aligned recurrent and convolutional layers are not required for a strong encoder-decoder on the tested translation tasks. Attention supplies the cross-position routing, while feed-forward transformations, normalization, residual paths, embeddings, positions, and autoregressive output generation remain essential.

Training parallelism should not be confused with fully parallel generation. The decoder mask allows known target positions to be trained together, but generation still predicts one target token at a time. The source also does not prove that its asymptotic comparison wins for every sequence length, architecture size, or hardware regime.

This explainer uses arXiv v7 as its canonical version and preserves the conflicting English-French results from the primary sources. Its visual is newly composed rather than copied: the arXiv record carries a non-exclusive distribution license, and the v7 PDF separately grants attributed reproduction of tables and figures only for journalistic or scholarly work.

claims.text: Attention Is All You Need was submitted to arXiv on June 12, 2017, appeared in NIPS 2017, and was last revised as arXiv v7 on August 2, 2023.

The Transformer replaces sequence-aligned recurrence and convolution with stacked self-attention and position-wise feed-forward processing in an encoder-decoder architecture, enabling more parallel training while retaining autoregressive decoding.
The base encoder and decoder each contain 6 layers; the decoder masks future target positions and adds encoder-decoder attention over the encoded source.
Scaled dot-product attention computes softmax of Q times K-transpose divided by the square root of the key dimension, then multiplies those weights by V.
The authors interpret multi-head attention as a way to attend jointly to different representation subspaces and positions instead of collapsing them through one attention average.

Table 1 gives full self-attention O(n squared times d) per-layer complexity, O(1) sequential operations, and O(1) maximum path length, while noting that restricted attention changes the path length to O(n divided by r).
The translation evaluation used about 4.5 million English-German and 36 million English-French sentence pairs on 8 P100 GPUs; the big model trained for 300,000 steps or 3.5 days.

ArXiv v7 reports 28.4 English-German BLEU and 41.8 English-French BLEU in its abstract and Table 2, but v7 prose and the proceedings PDF report 41.0 English-French BLEU while the NeurIPS landing abstract reports 41.1.
On newstest2013 development data, the base model reports 25.8 BLEU, the one-head variant 24.9, the 32-head variant 25.4, and the learned-position variant 25.7.
ArXiv v7 reports WSJ Section 23 parsing F1 of 91.3 with WSJ-only data and 92.7 with semi-supervised data, below the best listed result of 93.3.
The arXiv record uses a non-exclusive distribution license, while the v7 PDF separately permits attributed reproduction of its tables and figures solely for journalistic or scholarly works.

The paper does not establish fully parallel autoregressive generation, universal task superiority, reliable extrapolation of sinusoidal positions beyond training lengths, or attention weights as faithful causal explanations.

visuals.title: Long-range information takes different routes
visuals.question: How many sequential handoffs connect distant positions?

items.label: Recurrent route
items.detail: A distant dependency travels through a chain of hidden states, so the number of sequential handoffs grows with position distance.

items.label: Convolutional route
items.detail: Positions can be computed in parallel, but distant positions still require a path whose length grows with their separation.

items.label: Self-attention route
items.detail: Within one self-attention layer, any position can directly exchange information with every available position through one attention operation.

visuals.interpretation: The architectural motivation is path length: recurrence imposes a position-by-position chain, convolution shortens that chain, and global self-attention provides a direct within-layer route between positions.
limitations: The routes describe sequential computational path length, not measured wall-clock latency on every device.
The self-attention route does not imply fully parallel autoregressive generation.
visuals.alt_text: Three parallel lanes compare recurrent, convolutional, and self-attention routes between distant sequence positions. Recurrence uses a growing chain, convolution uses a shorter but distance-dependent path, and self-attention connects available positions within one layer.
visuals.fallback: Recurrence connects distant positions through a chain that grows with distance. Convolution can compute positions in parallel but still uses a distance-dependent path. Global self-attention allows direct exchange between available positions within one layer.

visuals.title: Attention replaces position mixing, not the whole layer
visuals.question: Which operations remain around self-attention in the Transformer stack?

items.label: Token and position representations
items.detail: Learned token vectors receive sinusoidal position encodings before entering the stack.

items.label: Multi-head self-attention
items.detail: Each position retrieves information from available positions through several learned attention heads.

items.label: Residual path and layer normalization
items.detail: A residual connection and normalization surround the attention sublayer.

items.label: Position-wise feed-forward network
items.detail: The same two-layer feed-forward transformation is applied independently at every position.

items.label: Residual path and layer normalization
items.detail: A second residual and normalization stage completes one encoder layer; the base encoder repeats this layer 6 times.

visuals.interpretation: The key substitution concerns how positions exchange information. Embeddings, positional information, feed-forward transformations, residual paths, and normalization remain essential parts of the repeated layer.
limitations: The stack shows one encoder layer and states the 6-layer repetition; it does not reproduce the paper's architecture figure.
Decoder masking and encoder-decoder attention are shown in the adjacent encoder-decoder visual.
visuals.alt_text: Layered architecture view: token and position representations enter multi-head self-attention, then a residual and normalization stage, then a position-wise feed-forward network, then another residual and normalization stage. The base encoder repeats this layer six times.
visuals.fallback: A Transformer encoder layer contains multi-head self-attention, a position-wise feed-forward network, two residual connections, and two normalization stages. Token embeddings and sinusoidal positions enter the stack, and the base encoder repeats the layer six times.

visuals.title: The two translation runs use different data scales
visuals.question: What training conditions sit behind the headline translation results?

items.label: English–German corpus
items.detail: About 4.5 million sentence pairs with a shared 37,000-token byte-pair vocabulary.

items.label: English–French corpus
items.detail: About 36 million sentence pairs with a 32,000-token word-piece vocabulary.

items.label: Base training run
items.detail: 100,000 steps, reported as about 12 hours on one machine with 8 NVIDIA P100 GPUs.

items.label: Big training run
items.detail: 300,000 steps, reported as about 3.5 days on the same stated hardware setup.

visuals.interpretation: The result table combines tasks and model runs built on substantially different corpus sizes, vocabularies, step counts, and training durations.
limitations: The view reports the paper's stated conditions and does not normalize compute across the two language pairs.
The durations are not repeated-run estimates and should not be treated as current hardware benchmarks.
visuals.alt_text: Four lanes list the English-German corpus of 4.5 million pairs and 37,000-token vocabulary, the English-French corpus of 36 million pairs and 32,000-token vocabulary, the 100,000-step 12-hour base run, and the 300,000-step 3.5-day big run on eight P100 GPUs.
visuals.fallback: English-German uses about 4.5 million pairs and a 37,000-token vocabulary; English-French uses about 36 million pairs and a 32,000-token vocabulary. On eight P100 GPUs, the base model reports 100,000 steps in 12 hours and the big model 300,000 steps in 3.5 days.

visuals.title: Global attention trades path length for quadratic pairings
visuals.question: What changes when attention is restricted for long sequences?

items.label: Full self-attention
items.detail: Every position can compare with every position: O(n²d) work per layer and a constant within-layer maximum path length.

items.label: Restricted attention proposal
items.detail: Limiting each position to a neighborhood can reduce pairings, but the paper notes that the maximum path grows to O(n/r).

visuals.interpretation: The same all-to-all connectivity that shortens paths also creates quadratic sequence-length cost. Restricting attention changes both the amount of comparison and the path-length property.
limitations: The paper presents restricted attention as future work rather than evaluating a complete long-context system.
Asymptotic expressions do not establish a universal wall-clock crossover on every architecture or device.
visuals.alt_text: Parallel comparison: full self-attention uses all pairwise position comparisons with O(n squared times d) cost and constant within-layer path length; restricted attention reduces pairings but increases maximum path length to O(n divided by r).
visuals.fallback: Full self-attention directly compares all positions, giving O(n squared times d) work and short paths. Restricting attention can reduce comparisons but increases the maximum path length to O(n divided by r).

visuals.title: The encoder and decoder do different attention work
visuals.question: How do the source sequence, known target prefix, and autoregressive output interact?

items.label: Source lane: encode known input
items.detail: The base encoder repeats 6 layers of multi-head self-attention and position-wise feed-forward processing over the source representations.

items.label: Target lane: hide the future
items.detail: The 6-layer decoder applies masked self-attention to the known target prefix so a target position cannot use later target positions.

items.label: Cross-lane retrieval
items.detail: Encoder-decoder attention uses decoder queries with keys and values from the encoded source, connecting each decoder position to source information.

items.label: Generation boundary
items.detail: Training can process known positions more parallelly under the mask, but generation remains autoregressive: one selected target token becomes part of the prefix before the next prediction.

visuals.interpretation: The Transformer removes sequence-aligned recurrence from the encoder-decoder's internal position mixing, but it does not erase the distinction between source encoding, masked target processing, and serial target generation. Attention routes information within and between the two lanes while feed-forward processing remains part of each stack.
limitations: This is a newly composed conceptual view, not a reproduction or close tracing of Figures 1 or 2.
The view collapses 6 repeated encoder and decoder layers and omits tensor shapes, residual paths, normalization, embeddings, positions, and the output projection.
It distinguishes training parallelism from autoregressive generation without claiming that every training operation is simultaneous.
It does not treat attention weights as faithful causal explanations or depict benchmark results.
visuals.alt_text: Parallel view of the original Transformer. A six-layer encoder uses self-attention and feed-forward processing over the source. A six-layer decoder masks future target positions, then uses decoder queries to attend to encoded-source keys and values. Known target positions can be processed in parallel during training, but generation appends one predicted token before predicting the next.
visuals.fallback: The encoder and decoder form related but distinct lanes. The 6-layer encoder processes the known source with self-attention and feed-forward layers. The 6-layer decoder masks future target positions and then attends to the encoded source. During training, known target positions can be evaluated in parallel under the mask. During generation, the selected target token must be appended before the next token is predicted.

visuals.title: Scaled dot-product attention is a sequence of four operations
visuals.question: How do queries, keys, and values become an attention output?

items.label: 1. Compare queries and keys
items.detail: Multiply Q by K-transpose to produce compatibility scores between each query and the available keys.

items.label: 2. Scale the scores
items.detail: Divide by the square root of the key dimension before normalization.

items.label: 3. Normalize with softmax
items.detail: Softmax converts the scaled compatibility scores into weights over the available positions.

items.label: 4. Mix the values
items.detail: Multiply the weights by V so the query receives a weighted mixture of value vectors.

items.label: Repeat in multiple heads
items.detail: Multi-head attention performs the operation in learned representation subspaces so the model can attend jointly to different positions and representations.

visuals.interpretation: Attention first measures query-key compatibility, controls its scale, turns scores into weights, and uses those weights to retrieve information from values. Multiple heads preserve several learned attention views instead of relying on one attention average.
limitations: The operation diagram omits tensor shapes, learned projection matrices, concatenation, and the final output projection.
A decoder causal mask restricts which positions are available before softmax; this diagram states the common core operation rather than drawing a specific mask.
Attention weights describe the model's computation but are not established as faithful causal explanations.
visuals.alt_text: Operation diagram for scaled dot-product attention: multiply queries by transposed keys, divide by the square root of the key dimension, apply softmax, and use the resulting weights to mix values. Multi-head attention repeats the operation in learned representation subspaces.
visuals.fallback: Scaled dot-product attention computes compatibility with Q times K-transpose, divides those scores by the square root of the key dimension, applies softmax, and multiplies the resulting weights by V. Multi-head attention repeats this calculation in learned representation subspaces so different positions and representations can be attended to jointly.

visuals.title: The English-French BLEU value depends on the source location
visuals.question: Which primary source reports 41.8, 41.0, or 41.1 BLEU?

items.label: arXiv v7 abstract
items.detail: Reports 41.8 English-French BLEU.

items.label: arXiv v7 Table 2
items.detail: Also reports 41.8 English-French BLEU.

items.label: arXiv v7 Section 6.1
items.detail: Reports 41.0 English-French BLEU in the prose.

items.label: Official proceedings PDF
items.detail: Reports 41.0 English-French BLEU.

items.label: NeurIPS proceedings landing page
items.detail: Reports 41.1 English-French BLEU in its abstract.

visuals.interpretation: There is no single source-independent English-French headline value in the cited primary record. A responsible citation must identify both the version and the exact location that supplies the number.
limitations: The matrix records the primary-source disagreement but does not determine which value the authors intended as canonical.
It does not compare preprocessing, decoding, or evaluation implementations beyond what the cited sources report.
The values should not be averaged or treated as independent experimental runs.
visuals.alt_text: Evidence matrix for conflicting English-French BLEU values. The arXiv v7 abstract and Table 2 report 41.8, arXiv v7 Section 6.1 and the official proceedings PDF report 41.0, and the NeurIPS proceedings landing-page abstract reports 41.1.
visuals.fallback: The English-French result differs across primary-source locations. ArXiv v7 reports 41.8 in its abstract and Table 2 but 41.0 in Section 6.1. The official proceedings PDF reports 41.0, while the NeurIPS landing-page abstract reports 41.1. The disagreement is unresolved, so citations must name the exact source and location.

visuals.title: The reported ablations cluster within one BLEU point
visuals.question: How do head count and learned positional embeddings compare with the base configuration on newstest2013?

items.label: Base configuration
items.detail: newstest2013 development BLEU

items.label: One attention head
items.detail: newstest2013 development BLEU

items.label: 32 attention heads
items.detail: newstest2013 development BLEU

items.label: Learned positional embeddings
items.detail: newstest2013 development BLEU

visuals.interpretation: In this development-set ablation, the base configuration reports the highest of the four listed values. Learned positional embeddings are close to the base result, while both the one-head and 32-head variants are lower.
limitations: The paper reports these values without repeated-seed uncertainty, so small differences should not be treated as variance-adjusted effects.
This plot compares only the four newstest2013 development results in the claim and does not generalize across tasks, datasets, or model sizes.
The learned-position result does not test reliable extrapolation beyond training sequence lengths.
visuals.alt_text: Dot plot of newstest2013 development BLEU: base configuration 25.8, one attention head 24.9, 32 attention heads 25.4, and learned positional embeddings 25.7. No repeated-seed uncertainty is reported.
visuals.fallback: On newstest2013 development data, the base configuration reports 25.8 BLEU. The one-head variant reports 24.9, the 32-head variant 25.4, and learned positional embeddings 25.7. These are single reported values without repeated-seed uncertainty.

glossary.definition: Mapping one sequence to another, such as translating a sentence from one language to another.
Attention in which queries, keys, and values come from representations of the same sequence, allowing positions in that sequence to exchange information.
Learned vector roles used by attention: a query requests information, keys determine compatibility with that request, and values carry the information that is mixed.
A restriction that prevents a decoder position from attending to future target positions, preserving autoregressive prediction.
A vector added to token embeddings to provide order information that the attention-only position-mixing mechanism does not supply by itself.
The translation-quality metric used by the paper for its WMT benchmark comparisons; values must be tied to the exact dataset, preprocessing, and source version.

revision_notes: Initial review-required explainer fixture based on arXiv 1706.03762 v7 dated August 2, 2023.
The explainer preserves the 41.8, 41.0, and 41.1 English-French BLEU contradiction across the arXiv v7 abstract and table, v7 prose, proceedings PDF, and proceedings landing page.
The mechanism appears before results in every reading path, and the review distinguishes parallel training from autoregressive generation.
The visual is an original source-grounded conceptual sequence and does not reproduce Figures 1 or 2; the arXiv distribution license and the PDF's narrower table-and-figure permission are recorded.
No claim of universal task superiority, reliable out-of-range positional extrapolation, or causal interpretability of attention weights is made.
